1: A type: Psalm 20 is a royal liturgy — the people's communal prayer before the king goes to battle. "May the LORD answer you in your day of trouble! May the name of the God of Jacob set you on high!" The community praying for the anointed king's victory is the type of the NT community praying for Christ's eschatological victory. The "day of trouble (yôm ṣārāh)" is the type of the hour of the Passion — the decisive battle for which the community prays.
2: A type: "May he send you help from his sanctuary and support you from Zion!" The divine help from the sanctuary sent to the battling king anticipates the Father's support of the Son in the Passion. Luke 22:43 ("An angel from heaven appeared to him and strengthened him") is a partial fulfillment — divine help sent from heaven to the one in the decisive battle. The full answer to Psalms 20:2 is the resurrection: help from the heavenly sanctuary that raised the king from death.
3: A type: "May he remember all your offerings and accept your burnt sacrifices!" The priestly dimension of the royal liturgy — the king's offerings accepted by YHWH — is fulfilled in Christ who is simultaneously the priest who offers and the sacrifice offered. Hebrews 9:14 ("how much more will the blood of Christ, who through the eternal Spirit offered himself unblemished to God, cleanse our consciences"). The "accepted sacrifice" of Psalms 20:3 is the type of the one sacrifice that is definitively acceptable.
4: A type: "May he grant you your heart's desire and fulfill all your plans!" The fulfillment of the king's desires and plans by YHWH is the covenant entitlement of the anointed. For Christ, this points to the fulfillment of the mission for which he came: John 17:4 ("I have brought you glory on earth by finishing the work you gave me to do"). The "heart's desire" of Christ — the redemption of the world, the glorification of the Father — is fulfilled through the cross and resurrection.
5: A type: "May we shout for joy at your victory, and in the name of our God raise our banners! May the LORD fulfill all your requests!" The community's anticipatory joy at the king's victory is the type of the NT community's celebration of Christ's accomplished victory. Revelation 5:12 ("Worthy is the Lamb, who was slain, to receive power and wealth and wisdom and strength and honor and glory and praise!") is the fulfillment of Psalm 20's shout of victory — the entire redeemed community celebrating the Messianic king's triumph.
6: A direct connection: "Now I know that the LORD saves his anointed one (mĕšîḥô); he answers him from his holy heaven with the saving power of his right hand." The Messiah term (māšîaḥ) appears here — the first use in a royal psalm of the specific word that will carry the weight of Israel's eschatological hopes. "The LORD saves his anointed" is the claim that the resurrection fulfills: God answered the crucified Messiah "from his holy heaven with the saving power of his right hand" — the right hand of God that raised Christ (Acts 2:33-34; Ephesians 1:20) is the "saving power of his right hand" of Psalms 20:6.
7: A revelation of God: "Some boast in chariots and some in horses, but we will boast in the name of the LORD our God." The anti-military boast principle — trust in divine power over human weapons — is the OT anticipation of Paul's theology of the cross: "For the foolishness of God is wiser than human wisdom, and the weakness of God is stronger than human strength" (1 Corinthians 1:25). The cross is the ultimate anti-chariot victory: the Messiah wins through what appears to be total defeat, confounding all worldly power calculations.
8: A theme: "They collapse and fall, but we rise and stand firm." The contrast between the fallers and the standers is the Psalm's confident assertion of Messianic victory. Romans 14:4 ("the Lord is able to make them stand") and Romans 8:31 ("If God is for us, who can be against us?") develop the confidence of Psalms 20:8. Those who trust in the Messiah's victory stand; those who oppose it collapse. The resurrection is the decisive demonstration that Christ, though he fell in death, stands eternally.
9: A direct connection: "LORD, give victory to the king! Answer us when we call." The community's closing petition for the king and for themselves — "answer us when we call" — is the prayer that the NT community prays through Christ as mediator. 1 Timothy 2:5 ("there is one God and one mediator between God and mankind, the man Christ Jesus"). The "answer us" of Psalms 20:9 is answered through the Messianic king who intercedes for his people and through whom the Father hears all prayer.
